content.text: Why Standard PRDs Fall Short for Claude Features

A standard Product Requirements Document covers what the feature does, who uses it, and what success looks like. For a deterministic software feature — a form, a search result, a data export — those three elements are sufficient. Claude-powered features require eight additional elements that do not appear in standard PRD templates.

A PRD without a system prompt specification, behavioural contracts, and an eval plan is not a specification for a Claude feature — it is a description of a feature's intent. The team will make incompatible assumptions and discover them in production.

The gap between standard and AI-specific PRDs is not incremental. It is structural: standard PRDs describe deterministic input-output mappings; Claude features require probabilistic behaviour contracts, evaluation methodology, and failure mode registries.

attrs.alt: PRD Section Map showing standard PRD sections mapped to AI-specific expansions and new AI-only sections including System Prompt Specification, Behavioural Contracts, Eval Plan, Token Budget, Failure Mode Registry, and Observability

content.text: Eight sections in an AI-specific PRD have no equivalent in standard templates. Four of the standard sections expand into AI-specific variants; four are entirely new:
Always — the system prompt is the product
When latency or cost SLAs are tight
When feature serves multiple trust levels
Always — defines what the model must and must not do

The system prompt is the most important engineering artefact in a Claude feature. It defines the model's role, constraints, and output format. Treating it as an implementation detail — something written by an engineer in an afternoon and never reviewed — is the most common source of production AI failures.

The system prompt specification section in the PRD must include: the full prompt text, a version identifier, the rationale for every non-obvious constraint, and an explicit list of what the prompt does NOT constrain (so reviewers know omissions are deliberate).
The PRD section template below shows the standard structure for all eight AI-specific sections:

# PRD: [Feature Name] — Claude Feature Specification

## 1. Overview & Problem Statement
[Standard PRD section — describe the user problem and the solution approach.]

## 2. Goals & Success Metrics
[Standard PRD section — list measurable goals.]

## 3. Personas & User Stories
[Standard PRD section — reference personas defined in the Persona Catalogue.]

---
## 4. System Prompt Specification   ← AI-specific

**Version:** [e.g. v1.0]
**Owner:** AI/ML team

```
[Full system prompt text here. This is a contract — changes require
a version bump and re-run of the eval suite.]
```

**Rationale for key constraints:**
- [Constraint 1]: [Why it was included, what failure mode it prevents]
- [Constraint 2]: [Why it was included]

**What this prompt does NOT constrain:** [Document intentional gaps
so reviewers know they are deliberate, not omissions.]

---
## 5. Model Selection & Configuration   ← AI-specific

| Parameter | Value | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Model | claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 | Latency < 3s for interactive review |
| max_tokens | 800 | Sufficient for clause analysis; prevents overlong responses |
| temperature | default (1.0) | Determinism not required; creative variation acceptable |
| streaming | true | Reduces perceived latency for long analyses |

---
## 6. Persona & Dimension Profile   ← AI-specific

[Reference persona IDs from the Persona Catalogue. Include the dimension
scores that drive eval thresholds for this feature.]

---
## 7. Behavioural Contracts   ← AI-specific

### Claude MUST
- [Concrete, testable behaviour]

### Claude MUST NOT
- [Concrete, testable constraint]

### Boundary Conditions
[Table of specific scenarios and expected behaviours — see template.]

### Non-Negotiable Constraints
[Safety-relevant constraints that cannot be relaxed by user instruction.]

---
## 8. Eval Plan & Acceptance Criteria   ← AI-specific

| Criterion ID | Criterion | Priority | Scenario Type | Judge |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AC-01 | [criterion text] | P0 | negative | LLM judge |

**Eval gate for launch:** All P0 criteria must pass at ≥ 95% on 100-sample suite.
P1 criteria must pass at ≥ 80%. P2 advisory only.

---
## 9. Context Window & Token Budget   ← AI-specific

[Include output of calculate_token_budget() here.]

**Chunking strategy (if required):** [Describe how large documents are split.]

---
## 10. Failure Mode Registry   ← AI-specific

[Include output of generate_failure_mode_registry() here, or link to the
failure mode registry artefact.]

---
## 11. Observability & Eval Instrumentation   ← AI-specific

**Logs:** [List what must be logged per request: token counts, latency,
persona ID, eval scores if real-time eval is enabled.]

**Metrics:** [List dashboard metrics: p50/p95 latency, refusal rate,
eval pass rate by criterion, error rate by failure category.]

**Alerts:** [List alert thresholds: eval pass rate < 90% → PagerDuty;
hallucination_risk failure rate > 1% → Slack #ai-incidents.]

---
## 12. Out of Scope
[Standard PRD section.]

## 13. Open Questions
[Standard PRD section — include open questions surfaced by AI sections.]
Generating AI-Specific PRD Sections with Claude

For teams shipping multiple Claude features, manually authoring all eight AI-specific sections for each feature is slow. The following utility generates all six content-heavy AI sections from a feature description, persona brief, and system prompt draft.

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

PRD_SECTION_TOOL = {
    "name": "write_prd_section",
    "description": "Write one section of a PRD for a Claude-powered feature.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "section_id":    {"type": "string"},
            "section_title": {"type": "string"},
            "content":       {"type": "string"},
            "open_questions":{"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}},
            "reviewers":     {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}}
        },
        "required": ["section_id","section_title","content","open_questions","reviewers"]
    }
}

AI_SECTIONS = [
    ("system_prompt_spec",   "System Prompt Specification"),
    ("model_config",         "Model Selection & Configuration"),
    ("behavioural_contract", "Behavioural Contracts"),
    ("eval_plan",            "Eval Plan & Acceptance Criteria"),
    ("token_budget",         "Context Window & Token Budget"),
    ("failure_mode_registry","Failure Mode Registry"),
]

def generate_ai_prd_sections(
    feature_description: str,
    persona_brief: str,
    system_prompt_draft: str
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Generate all AI-specific PRD sections for a Claude-powered feature.
    Returns a list of section dicts in document order.
    """
    sections = []
    for section_id, section_title in AI_SECTIONS:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=1000,
            tools=[PRD_SECTION_TOOL],
            tool_choice={"type": "any"},
            messages=[{
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"Feature: {feature_description}\n"
                    f"Persona: {persona_brief}\n"
                    f"System prompt draft: {system_prompt_draft[:300]}...\n\n"
                    f"Write the '{section_title}' section of the PRD. "
                    "Be concrete — avoid placeholder text. "
                    "List open questions this section leaves unresolved."
                )
            }]
        )
        for block in response.content:
            if block.type == "tool_use" and block.name == "write_prd_section":
                sections.append(block.input)
    return sections

# Example
sections = generate_ai_prd_sections(
    feature_description="Claude-powered contract review assistant for solo legal practitioners",
    persona_brief="Maya Chen, IP attorney, zero tolerance for hallucinated citations, expert fluency",
    system_prompt_draft=(
        "You are a legal analysis assistant. Help legal professionals review contracts "
        "by identifying non-standard clauses. Always cite exact contract language. "
        "Rate risk as High/Medium/Low. Never provide binding legal advice."
    )
)
for s in sections:
    print(f"\n## {s['section_title']}")
    print(s['content'][:200], "...")
    if s['open_questions']:
        print("Open questions:", s['open_questions'])

The behavioural contract is the most critical section for zero-tolerance personas. It specifies, in plain language and in table form, exactly what Claude must do, must not do, and how it must behave in specific boundary scenarios. The contract drives the eval plan — every must/must-not becomes at least one eval test.

Behavioural contracts should be written by the AI/ML engineer and reviewed by Legal, Product, and Security before the PRD is approved. The following template shows the structure for a legal contract review assistant:

# behavioural_contract.md (template)
# Include verbatim in PRD Section 4: Behavioural Contracts

## Claude MUST

- Cite the exact contract language for every non-standard clause it identifies.
- Rate clause risk as exactly one of: High / Medium / Low.
- Decline requests for binding legal opinions with a specific disclaimer phrase.
- Acknowledge user corrections within the same response turn.
- Remain within the scope of the supplied contract excerpt.

## Claude MUST NOT

- Fabricate clause text not present in the supplied document.
- Provide a definitive enforceability ruling.
- Claim to be a licensed attorney or offer to represent the user.
- Silently ignore a clause the user has explicitly asked about.
- Apply advice from one jurisdiction's law to another without flagging the difference.

## Boundary Conditions

| Scenario | Expected Behaviour |
|---|---|
| User asks about a clause not in the excerpt | Acknowledge the clause is not visible in the supplied text; ask user to provide it |
| User asks for a yes/no enforceability ruling | Decline; suggest consulting a qualified attorney |
| User corrects a missed clause | Acknowledge the correction; re-analyse with the missed clause included |
| Document exceeds context window | Surface a warning before analysis; advise chunking |
| Ambiguous clause interpretation | Present both interpretations; flag as a legal ambiguity |

## Non-Negotiable Constraints

These constraints cannot be relaxed by user instruction or system prompt override:
1. Never provide binding legal advice.
2. Never claim to be a licensed attorney.
3. Never fabricate case law citations.

The eval plan section of the PRD specifies, before any code is written, what the test suite must prove before the feature can launch. Each acceptance criterion maps to one or more eval scenarios with a test input, pass/fail criteria, and a judge type (exact match, LLM judge, or human review).

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

EVAL_SPEC_TOOL = {
    "name": "write_eval_spec",
    "description": "Write a concrete eval specification for one acceptance criterion.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "criterion_id":   {"type": "string"},
            "criterion":      {"type": "string"},
            "scenario_type":  {"type": "string", "enum": ["happy_path","boundary","negative","recovery"]},
            "priority":       {"type": "string", "enum": ["P0","P1","P2"]},
            "test_input":     {"type": "string"},
            "pass_criteria":  {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}},
            "fail_criteria":  {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}},
            "judge_type":     {"type": "string", "enum": ["exact_match","llm_judge","human_review"]}
        },
        "required": ["criterion_id","criterion","scenario_type","priority",
                     "test_input","pass_criteria","fail_criteria","judge_type"]
    }
}

ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA = [
    "Claude cites exact contract language for every non-standard clause identified",
    "Claude declines to provide binding legal advice when asked",
    "Claude acknowledges and corrects missed clauses when user provides feedback",
    "Claude does not fabricate clause content not present in supplied text",
]

def generate_eval_plan(acceptance_criteria: list[str], feature_description: str) -> list[dict]:
    """
    For each acceptance criterion, generate a concrete eval specification
    with a test input, pass/fail criteria, and judge type.
    """
    specs = []
    for i, criterion in enumerate(acceptance_criteria):
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=600,
            tools=[EVAL_SPEC_TOOL],
            tool_choice={"type": "any"},
            messages=[{
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"Feature: {feature_description}\n"
                    f"Acceptance criterion #{i+1}: {criterion}\n\n"
                    "Write a concrete eval specification. The test_input must be a realistic "
                    "user message, not a placeholder. Assign P0 for safety/accuracy criteria, "
                    "P1 for quality, P2 for nice-to-have."
                )
            }]
        )
        for block in response.content:
            if block.type == "tool_use" and block.name == "write_eval_spec":
                specs.append(block.input)
    return specs

specs = generate_eval_plan(
    ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA,
    "Claude-powered contract review assistant for solo legal practitioners"
)
for s in specs:
    print(f"[{s['priority']}] {s['criterion_id']}: {s['criterion'][:60]}")
    print(f"  Judge: {s['judge_type']} | Scenario: {s['scenario_type']}")

Context window failures are silent — when a document is truncated by the context limit, the model does not flag this. The token budget section of the PRD must quantify the expected token usage for typical and worst-case documents and specify a chunking strategy when worst-case usage exceeds the safety limit.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def calculate_token_budget(
    system_prompt: str,
    sample_documents: list[str],
    conversation_turns: int = 5,
    response_tokens: int = 800,
    model_context_limit: int = 200_000,
    safety_margin: float = 0.85
) -> dict:
    """
    Calculate token budget for a Claude-powered feature.

    Returns a budget spec suitable for inclusion in the PRD Token Budget section.
    Uses the rough 4-chars-per-token heuristic — replace with Anthropic's
    token-counting API endpoint for production accuracy.
    """
    system_tokens = len(system_prompt) // 4

    # Estimate typical and max document sizes
    doc_tokens = [len(d) // 4 for d in sample_documents]
    avg_doc_tokens = sum(doc_tokens) // len(doc_tokens) if doc_tokens else 0
    max_doc_tokens = max(doc_tokens) if doc_tokens else 0

    # Conversation overhead: history grows each turn
    # Rough: (system + doc) + conversation_turns * avg_turn_tokens
    avg_turn_tokens = 200  # user message estimate
    conversation_overhead = conversation_turns * (avg_turn_tokens + response_tokens)

    total_typical = system_tokens + avg_doc_tokens + conversation_overhead
    total_max = system_tokens + max_doc_tokens + conversation_overhead
    safe_limit = int(model_context_limit * safety_margin)

    budget = {
        "model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        "context_limit": model_context_limit,
        "safety_limit": safe_limit,
        "system_prompt_tokens": system_tokens,
        "avg_document_tokens": avg_doc_tokens,
        "max_document_tokens": max_doc_tokens,
        "conversation_overhead_tokens": conversation_overhead,
        "total_typical_tokens": total_typical,
        "total_max_tokens": total_max,
        "status": (
            "OK" if total_max < safe_limit
            else "WARN: max scenario exceeds safety limit — chunking required"
        ),
        "chunking_required": total_max >= safe_limit,
        "max_safe_document_tokens": safe_limit - system_tokens - conversation_overhead,
    }
    return budget

# Example
SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a legal analysis assistant. Review contracts for
non-standard clauses. Always cite exact language. Never provide binding legal advice."""

sample_docs = [
    "A" * 40_000,   # 40K chars ≈ 10K tokens — typical NDA
    "A" * 200_000,  # 200K chars ≈ 50K tokens — large M&A agreement
]

budget = calculate_token_budget(SYSTEM_PROMPT, sample_docs)
print(f"System prompt: {budget['system_prompt_tokens']:,} tokens")
print(f"Typical doc: {budget['avg_document_tokens']:,} tokens")
print(f"Max doc: {budget['max_document_tokens']:,} tokens")
print(f"Conversation overhead (5 turns): {budget['conversation_overhead_tokens']:,} tokens")
print(f"Status: {budget['status']}")
print(f"Max safe document: {budget['max_safe_document_tokens']:,} tokens")
if budget['chunking_required']:
    print("ACTION REQUIRED: Document chunking strategy must be defined in PRD.")

The failure mode registry is the bridge between the behavioural contract (what must not happen) and the eval plan (how we verify it does not). Each entry in the registry links a failure mode to its trigger, user impact, severity, mitigation, and the specific eval test that covers it.

import anthropic, json
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

FAILURE_RECORD_TOOL = {
    "name": "record_prd_failure_mode",
    "description": "Record a failure mode for the PRD failure mode registry.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "id":            {"type": "string"},
            "category":      {"type": "string"},
            "description":   {"type": "string"},
            "trigger":       {"type": "string"},
            "user_impact":   {"type": "string"},
            "severity":      {"type": "string", "enum": ["critical","high","medium","low"]},
            "mitigation":    {"type": "string"},
            "eval_test":     {"type": "string"},
            "owner":         {"type": "string"}
        },
        "required": ["id","category","description","trigger","user_impact",
                     "severity","mitigation","eval_test","owner"]
    }
}

def generate_failure_mode_registry(
    feature_description: str,
    behavioural_contract: str
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Populate the PRD failure mode registry by asking Claude to enumerate
    failure modes that would violate the behavioural contract.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=2500,
        tools=[FAILURE_RECORD_TOOL],
        tool_choice={"type": "auto"},
        system=(
            "You are a reliability engineer specialising in AI features. "
            "For each must/must-not in the behavioural contract, identify at least "
            "one failure mode. Be specific — describe the trigger condition, not "
            "just the general category."
        ),
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Feature: {feature_description}\n\n"
                f"Behavioural contract:\n{behavioural_contract}\n\n"
                "Identify all failure modes that would violate a must or must-not."
            )
        }]
    )
    return [b.input for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use" and b.name == "record_prd_failure_mode"]

# Brief behavioural contract summary for the generator
contract_summary = """
MUST: cite exact contract language; rate risk as High/Medium/Low; decline binding advice.
MUST NOT: fabricate clause text; give enforceability rulings; claim attorney status.
"""
modes = generate_failure_mode_registry(
    "Claude contract review assistant for solo legal practitioners",
    contract_summary
)
print(f"Generated {len(modes)} failure modes for registry")
for m in sorted(modes, key=lambda x: ["critical","high","medium","low"].index(x["severity"])):
    print(f"  [{m['severity'].upper():8}] {m['id']}: {m['description'][:60]}")
Before a Claude feature PRD is approved, all eight AI-specific sections should pass a structured review. The following checklist can be run as a CI gate on the PRD document:
: version identifier present; rationale documented for every constraint; non-negotiable constraints identified.
: must/must-not lists are concrete and testable (not aspirational); boundary condition table covers at least 5 scenarios; non-negotiable constraints listed separately.
: every must/must-not has at least one eval test; P0 tests identified; launch gate thresholds specified (e.g. 95% pass rate on 100-sample suite).
: worst-case token count calculated; chunking strategy defined if worst case exceeds 85% of context limit.
: every must/must-not maps to at least one failure mode; every failure mode has an eval_test linked; every CRITICAL/HIGH failure has a documented mitigation.
: token count, latency, and eval score are logged per request; alert thresholds defined for eval pass rate degradation.

Standard PRD templates are insufficient for Claude-powered features because they lack the structures needed to specify probabilistic behaviour: system prompt specifications, behavioural contracts, eval plans, token budgets, failure mode registries, and observability instrumentation. These eight AI-specific sections are not optional additions — they are the specification artefacts that determine whether the team builds the right thing, and whether they can verify that they built it correctly. Generating them with structured tool use ensures completeness and makes them machine-readable for downstream pipeline automation.
